subject: physics
description: Calculus-based: Mechanics (Kinematics, Newton's Laws, Work/Energy, Systems of Particles, Rotation, Oscillations, Gravitation) + E&M (Electrostatics, Conductors, Circuits, Magnetic Fields, Electromagnetism).

domains.name: 力学-功能动量 Mechanics: Energy/Momentum

nodes.name: 功的积分定义
curriculum_points: 1.2.C.1.i Instantaneous velocity is the rate of change of the object’s position, which is equal to the derivative of position with respect to time. Relevant equations: v = dr/dt, v_x = dx/dt
1.2.C.1.ii Instantaneous acceleration is the rate of change of the object’s velocity, which is equal to the derivative of velocity with respect to time. Relevant equations: a = dv/dt, a_x = dv_x/dt
1.2.C.2 Time-dependent functions and instantaneous values of position, velocity, and acceleration can be determined using differentiation and integration.

1.3.A.4.i An object’s instantaneous velocity is the rate of change of the object’s position, which is equal to the slope of a line tangent to a point on a graph of the object’s position as a function of time. Relevant equation: v_x = dx/dt

1.3.A.4.ii An object’s instantaneous acceleration is the rate of change of the object’s velocity, which is equal to the slope of a line tangent to a point on a graph of the object’s velocity as a function of time. Relevant equation: a_x = dv_x/dt

nodes.name: 质心运动
curriculum_points:
2.5.A.2
Newton’s second law of motion states that the acceleration of a system’s center of mass has a magnitude proportional to the magnitude of the net force exerted on the system and is in the same direction as that net force.
Relevant equation:
∑F

net

2.5.A.3
The velocity of a system’s center of mass will only change if a nonzero net external force is exerted on that system.

nodes.name: 动量与冲量
curriculum_points:
1.5 Motion in Two or Three Dimensions
Required Course Content
LEARNING OBJECTIVE ESSENTIAL KNOWLEDGE
1.5.A 1.5.A.1
Describe the motion of an Motion in two or three dimensions can be analyzed using one-dimensional kinematic relationships if the motion is separated into components.
1.5.A.4
Projectile motion is a special case of two-dimensional motion that has zero acceleration in one dimension and constant, nonzero acceleration in the second dimension.

TOPIC 1.5
Motion in Two or Three Dimensions

1.5.A 1.5.A.1
Describe the motion of an Motion in two or three dimensions can be analyzed using one-dimensional kinematic relationships if the motion is separated into components.
1.5.A.4
Projectile motion is a special case of two-dimensional motion that has zero acceleration in one dimension and constant, nonzero acceleration in the second dimension.

domains.name: 力学-转动与振动 Mechanics: Rotation/Oscillation

nodes.name: 转动惯量
curriculum_points:
1.2.A.1
When using the object model, the size, shape, and internal configuration are ignored. The object may be treated as a single point with extensive properties such as mass and charge.

2.1.A.2
If the properties or interactions of the constituent objects within a system are not important in modeling the behavior of the macroscopic system, the system can itself be treated as a single object.

2.1.A.4
Individual objects within a chosen system may behave differently from each other as well as from the system as a whole.

2.1.A.5
The internal structure of a system affects the analysis of that system.

nodes.name: 转动动力学
curriculum_points:
1.5.A.4
Projectile motion is a special case of two-dimensional motion that has zero acceleration in one dimension and constant, nonzero acceleration in the second dimension.

BOUNDARY STATEMENT
AP Physics C: Mechanics only expects students to quantitatively analyze the motion of an object in two dimensions. AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism expects students to also qualitatively describe the motion of a particle in three dimensions.

nodes.name: 简谐振动(微分方程)
curriculum_points: 1.1.A.1 Scalars are quantities described by magnitude only; vectors are quantities described by both magnitude and direction.
1.1.A.2 Vectors can be visually modeled as arrows with appropriate direction and lengths proportional to their magnitude.
1.1.A.3 Distance and speed are examples of scalar quantities, while position, displacement, velocity, and acceleration are examples of vector quantities.
1.1.A.4 Vectors can be expressed in unit vector notation or as a magnitude and a direction.

nodes.name: 万有引力与轨道
curriculum_points:
1.2.A.1
When using the object model, the size, shape, and internal configuration are ignored. The object may be treated as a single point with extensive properties such as mass and charge.

2.1.A.1
System properties are determined by the interactions between objects within the system.

2.1.A.2
If the properties or interactions of the constituent objects within a system are not important in modeling the behavior of the macroscopic system, the system can itself be treated as a single object.

2.1.A.3
Systems may allow interactions between constituent parts of the system and the environment, which may result in the transfer of energy or mass.

nodes.name: 高斯定律
curriculum_points:
1.5.A.1
Motion in two or three dimensions can be analyzed using one-dimensional kinematic relationships if the motion is separated into components.

1.5.A.2
Velocity and acceleration may be different in each dimension and may be nonuniform.

1.5.A.4
Projectile motion is a special case of two-dimensional motion that has zero acceleration in one dimension and constant, nonzero acceleration in the second dimension.

domains.name: 电磁-磁场与电磁感应 E&M: Magnetism

nodes.name: 磁场与洛伦兹力
curriculum_points: 1.1.A.1 Scalars are quantities described by magnitude only; vectors are quantities described by both magnitude and direction.
1.1.A.2 Vectors can be visually modeled as arrows with appropriate direction and lengths proportional to their magnitude.
1.1.A.3 Distance and speed are examples of scalar quantities, while position, displacement, velocity, and acceleration are examples of vector quantities.
1.1.A.4 Vectors can be expressed in unit vector notation or as a magnitude and a direction.

nodes.name: 毕奥-萨伐尔定律
curriculum_points: 2.2.A.1 Forces are vector quantities that describe the interactions between objects or systems.
2.2.A.1.i A force exerted on an object or system is always due to the interaction of that object or system with another object or system.
2.2.A.1.ii An object or system cannot exert a net force on itself.

nodes.name: 安培定律
curriculum_points:

Motion in two or three dimensions can be analyzed using one-dimensional kinematic relationships if the motion is separated into components.

1.5.A.4
Projectile motion is a special case of two-dimensional motion that has zero acceleration in one dimension and constant, nonzero acceleration in the second dimension.
